title: Expressing support for the Geneva Consensus Declaration on Promoting Women's Health and Strengthening the Family and urging that the United States rejoin this historic declaration.
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.
plain_summary: This congressional resolution supports an international agreement called the Geneva Consensus Declaration that focuses on women's health and family issues. The declaration says there is no international right to abortion and that families are very important to society. President Biden removed the United States from this agreement, but Congress wants America to rejoin it. The resolution also says the US should not pay for or support abortions or abortion lobbying in other countries.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Foreign Affairs.

sections.title: Preamble - Supporting Statements
sections.text: The United States strongly supports women getting the best possible health care and well-being throughout their lives. A group of different countries created the Geneva Consensus Declaration on Promoting Women's Health and Strengthening the Family. This group was committed to finding better ways to improve women's health, protect families as the foundation of healthy societies, value life at all stages, and respect each country's right to make its own laws about these values without pressure from other countries. Thirty-two countries from around the world signed this declaration on October twenty-second, two thousand twenty. These countries represent more than one billion six hundred million people. Now thirty-nine countries are part of this group. The United States was the main supporter of this declaration when Donald Trump was president. When Joseph Biden became president, he removed the United States from signing this declaration, at least for now. However, federal laws that stop the United States from doing or paying for abortions, abortion lobbying, or forced family planning in other countries are still in effect. The Geneva Consensus Declaration states that all people are equal before the law and that women's human rights are an essential part of all human rights and basic freedoms. The declaration also states that every person has dignity and worth and that every human being has the basic right to life. It says there is no international right to abortion and no international requirement for countries to pay for or help with abortions. The declaration states that the family is the natural and basic unit of society and deserves protection from society and the government. The countries that signed this declaration work together to make sure no country gets bullied, isolated, or silenced about the core values in the declaration.

sections.title: Congressional Resolution
sections.text: Congress agrees with the commitments to improve women's health and protect life and family that are stated in the Geneva Consensus Declaration on Promoting Women's Health and Strengthening the Family. Congress praises the countries that signed this declaration for their dedication to improving women's health, protecting life at every stage while stating there is no international right to abortion, and supporting the importance of family as the foundation of society. Congress declares that the principles about women's health, the dignity of every life, and family that are recognized in this declaration remain true for everyone. Congress welcomes chances to strengthen support for this declaration. Congress will defend every country's right to adopt national policies that promote women's health, protect the right to life, and strengthen the family, as written in this declaration. Congress will work with the executive branch of the United States government to make sure the United States does not conduct or pay for abortions, abortion lobbying, or forced family planning in other countries, following longstanding federal law. Congress urges the countries that signed this declaration to defend the universal principles about valuing every life and family that are expressed in the declaration.
